name: auth
context: MCP authorization — OAuth 2.1 flows, token handling, and security for remote MCP servers over HTTP
info: This context node covers MCP authorization for HTTP-based transports. MCP uses OAuth 2.1 for authorizing client access to protected remote servers. The MCP server acts as an OAuth resource server; the MCP client acts as an OAuth client. Authorization servers provide token issuance via standard OAuth flows with PKCE. Discovery uses OAuth 2.0 Protected Resource Metadata (RFC 9728) and Authorization Server Metadata (RFC 8414). Dynamic Client Registration (RFC 7591) enables seamless server discovery. Tokens are passed as Bearer tokens in the Authorization header. Stdio-based servers skip OAuth and use environment credentials instead.
body:
# MCP Authorization

Authorization is optional in MCP but recommended for remote servers. When supported, HTTP-based transports use OAuth 2.1. Stdio-based servers retrieve credentials from the environment instead.

## Roles

| MCP Role | OAuth Role |
|----------|------------|
| MCP Server | OAuth Resource Server — accepts access tokens |
| MCP Client | OAuth Client — obtains and presents tokens |
| Authorization Server | Issues access tokens (may be co-located with the MCP server or separate) |

## Authorization Flow

### 1. Discovery

Client makes a request without a token. Server returns `401 Unauthorized` with a `WWW-Authenticate` header pointing to its resource metadata:

```http
HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized
WWW-Authenticate: Bearer resource_metadata="https://mcp.example.com/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource"
```

### 2. Protected Resource Metadata (RFC 9728)

Client fetches `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource` from the MCP server:

```json
{
  "resource": "https://mcp.example.com",
  "authorization_servers": ["https://auth.example.com"]
}
```

### 3. Authorization Server Metadata (RFC 8414)

Client fetches `/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server` from the auth server to discover endpoints:

```json
{
  "issuer": "https://auth.example.com",
  "authorization_endpoint": "https://auth.example.com/authorize",
  "token_endpoint": "https://auth.example.com/token",
  "registration_endpoint": "https://auth.example.com/register"
}
```

### 4. Dynamic Client Registration (RFC 7591)

If supported, the client registers automatically:

```http
POST https://auth.example.com/register
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "client_name": "My MCP Client",
  "redirect_uris": ["http://localhost:3000/callback"],
  "grant_types": ["authorization_code"],
  "response_types": ["code"]
}
```

### 5. Authorization Code Flow with PKCE

1. Client generates PKCE `code_verifier` and `code_challenge`
2. Client opens browser to authorization endpoint with `code_challenge` and `resource` parameter
3. User authenticates and authorizes
4. Auth server redirects with authorization code
5. Client exchanges code + `code_verifier` for access token
6. Client includes `resource` parameter in token request (RFC 8707)

### 6. Authenticated Requests

All subsequent MCP requests include the Bearer token:

```http
POST /mcp HTTP/1.1
Host: mcp.example.com
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIs...
MCP-Protocol-Version: 2025-06-18
Mcp-Session-Id: abc123
Content-Type: application/json

{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "method": "tools/list"}
```

## Token Handling Rules

- Tokens must be included in **every** HTTP request (even within the same session)
- Tokens must **not** be sent in the URL query string
- Servers must validate that tokens were issued specifically for them (audience validation)
- Servers must **never** pass through tokens to upstream APIs
- Invalid/expired tokens receive HTTP 401
- Insufficient permissions receive HTTP 403

## Stdio Transport Auth

Stdio servers do **not** use OAuth. Instead, credentials come from the environment:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "database": {
      "command": "python",
      "args": ["db_server.py"],
      "env": {
        "DATABASE_URL": "postgresql://user:pass@localhost/db",
        "API_KEY": "sk-..."
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## Security Requirements

- All auth server endpoints must use HTTPS
- All redirect URIs must be localhost or HTTPS
- Clients must implement PKCE (prevents authorization code interception)
- Clients must include the `resource` parameter (RFC 8707) to bind tokens to specific servers
- Servers must validate token audience to prevent confused deputy attacks
- Auth servers should issue short-lived access tokens
- Public clients must use refresh token rotation

## Error Codes

| Status | Meaning |
|--------|----------|
| 401 | Token missing, invalid, or expired |
| 403 | Token valid but insufficient permissions |
| 400 | Malformed authorization request |

sections.title: Authorization server discovery
sections.body: MCP servers implement RFC 9728 (Protected Resource Metadata) to advertise their authorization servers. Clients get a 401 with `WWW-Authenticate` header, fetch `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource`, then fetch `/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server` from the discovered auth server. Dynamic Client Registration (RFC 7591) allows automatic client setup.

sections.title: OAuth 2.1 authorization flow
sections.body: MCP uses the OAuth 2.1 authorization code flow with PKCE. Client generates code_verifier/code_challenge, redirects user to auth server, receives authorization code, exchanges it for an access token. The `resource` parameter (RFC 8707) binds tokens to specific MCP servers. All endpoints must be HTTPS.

sections.title: Access token handling
sections.body: Tokens are sent as `Authorization: Bearer <token>` in every HTTP request. Servers validate tokens per OAuth 2.1 Section 5.2, checking audience, expiration, and scope. Tokens must not be passed through to upstream APIs. Invalid tokens get 401; insufficient permissions get 403. Auth servers should issue short-lived tokens with refresh token rotation for public clients.

sections.title: Security considerations
sections.body: Key security requirements: HTTPS for all auth endpoints, PKCE mandatory, resource parameter mandatory (RFC 8707), token audience validation, no token passthrough. Servers must validate Origin header to prevent DNS rebinding. Local servers should bind to 127.0.0.1 only. State parameters prevent CSRF. Redirect URIs must be pre-registered.

links.context: Transport layer where authorization is implemented
Servers implement the resource server side
Clients implement the OAuth client side
Architecture context for understanding auth placement

related.note: Authorization applies to the Streamable HTTP transport layer
Servers must validate tokens when acting as resource servers
Clients must implement OAuth flows to obtain tokens
